Sam: Welcome back to Cross-Show. Today we're talking about systems thinking, and I'm joined by Dr. Elena Fischer, returning systems thinker. Dr. Elena Fischer, thanks for being here.
Dr. Elena Fischer: Thanks, Sam. Excited for this one — systems thinking is something I've been thinking about a lot.
Sam: Today's episode is sponsored by Notion. Notion replaces the dozen tools your team is half-using. Try notion.com today.
Dr. Elena Fischer: In our 2024 conversation I was more optimistic about top-down interventions. I've revised that — the work happens at the seams.
Sam: That's a real shift. Worth pulling on.I want to dig into that. How does Bloomberg fit into this picture?
Dr. Elena Fischer: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Two years of systems-thinking work has made me more humble about leverage points. We've seen this play out at teams using IPCC. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Sam: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Dr. Elena Fischer: It comes down to this: Reliability and sustainability share more vocabulary than I used to admit. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Sam: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: Today's episode is sponsored by Linear. Linear is the issue tracker built for speed — keyboard-first. Visit linear.com/podcast for a free trial.
Sam: Welcome back to the show.What does the failure mode look like? What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Dr. Elena Fischer: Yeah, so — Two years of systems-thinking work has made me more humble about leverage points. We've seen this play out at teams using Bloomberg. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Sam: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. Where do sustainability and systems thinking intersect?
Dr. Elena Fischer: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Reliability and sustainability share more vocabulary than I used to admit. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Sam: Let's pivot — reliability is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Dr. Elena Fischer: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around reliability cross-cut everything we've covered.
Sam: That's a useful framing. How does Bloomberg fit into this picture?
Dr. Elena Fischer: It comes down to this: Two years of systems-thinking work has made me more humble about leverage points. We've seen this play out at teams using IPCC. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Sam: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. What's the most underrated piece of systems thinking?
Dr. Elena Fischer: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Reliability and sustainability share more vocabulary than I used to admit. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Sam: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Dr. Elena Fischer: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.
